summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the most common method of solid waste disposal in the United States?

Sanitary landfills are the most common solid waste disposal method in the United States, receiving approximately 50% of all municipal solid waste, though recycling and composting rates have been increasing.

What is the waste management hierarchy from most to least preferred?

The waste management hierarchy prioritizes source reduction (prevention) as most preferred, followed by reuse, recycling/composting, energy recovery (waste-to-energy), and disposal (landfill) as the least preferred option.

What is a major environmental concern associated with landfills?

Landfills produce leachate (contaminated liquid) that can seep into groundwater and methane gas from anaerobic decomposition of organic waste, which is a potent greenhouse gas approximately 80 times more warming than CO₂ over 20 years.

What is e-waste?

E-waste (electronic waste) consists of discarded electronic devices containing valuable materials (gold, copper) and hazardous substances (lead, mercury, cadmium). It is the fastest-growing waste stream globally.

What is the benefit of waste-to-energy incineration?

Waste-to-energy incineration reduces solid waste volume by up to 90% and generates electricity or heat from the combustion process, though it still produces ash that requires landfill disposal and must control air emissions.

What is the difference between open dumps and sanitary landfills?

Sanitary landfills are engineered with clay and synthetic liners to prevent groundwater contamination, leachate collection systems, methane gas capture, daily soil cover, and monitoring wells, none of which are present in open dumps.

What is the largest component of municipal solid waste by weight in the United States?

Paper and paperboard make up the largest component of municipal solid waste by weight in the United States at approximately 23%, followed by food waste and yard trimmings.

What is composting?

Composting is the controlled aerobic decomposition of organic materials (food scraps, yard waste) by microorganisms into humus, a nutrient-rich soil amendment. It diverts organic waste from landfills and reduces methane emissions.

What is 'municipal solid waste (MSW)'?

Municipal solid waste (MSW) includes everyday items discarded by households, businesses, and institutions such as packaging, food scraps, furniture, clothing, bottles, newspapers, appliances, and yard trimmings.

What is 'leachate'?

Leachate is liquid that percolates through waste in a landfill, dissolving and carrying harmful substances such as heavy metals, organic compounds, and pathogens. Modern landfills collect leachate through drainage systems for treatment.

What is a 'sanitary landfill'?

A sanitary landfill is an engineered facility designed to isolate waste from the surrounding environment using impermeable liners, leachate collection and treatment systems, methane gas management, daily soil cover, and groundwater monitoring.

What is a 'circular economy'?

A circular economy moves away from the linear 'take-make-dispose' model by designing products for durability, reuse, and recyclability, keeping materials circulating in the economy and minimizing waste sent to landfills.

Researchers find that approximately 8 million metric tons of plastic enter the world's oceans each year. Most of this plastic originates from land-based sources. What is the most effective strategy to address ocean plastic pollution?

Preventing plastic from reaching the ocean is far more effective than cleanup. This requires reducing single-use plastics, improving waste collection infrastructure especially in developing coastal nations, and designing alternatives to disposable plastic products.

A city compares two waste disposal options: a new waste-to-energy incinerator or expanding the existing landfill. The incinerator would reduce waste volume by 90% and generate electricity, but some community members oppose it due to air quality concerns. What is the main trade-off?

Waste-to-energy incineration dramatically reduces waste volume and generates renewable electricity but produces air pollutants (even with modern controls), toxic fly ash requiring hazardous waste disposal, and bottom ash for landfill disposal.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: What is the waste management hierarchy from most to least preferred?

The waste management hierarchy prioritizes source reduction (prevention) as most preferred, followed by reuse, recycling/composting, energy recovery (waste-to-energy), and disposal (landfill) as the least preferred option.

Example: What percentage of municipal solid waste in the United States is recycled or composted?

Approximately 32% of municipal solid waste in the United States is recycled or composted, according to EPA data. This rate has been relatively stable, with significant room for improvement compared to leading countries.

Example: What does 'source reduction' mean in waste management?

Source reduction (waste prevention) is the most preferred strategy in the waste hierarchy. It involves designing products with less material, reducing packaging, buying in bulk, and making choices that prevent waste generation.

Example: What does 'cradle-to-grave' mean in the context of hazardous waste management?

Cradle-to-grave is the RCRA's approach to hazardous waste management, requiring documentation and proper handling at every stage from generation (cradle) to final disposal (grave), ensuring accountability throughout the waste's lifecycle.

Example: What does 'integrated waste management' mean?

Integrated waste management uses a combination of approaches, applying the most appropriate methods for different waste streams according to the waste hierarchy, rather than relying on a single disposal method.

Example: What does 'tipping fee' mean in waste management?

A tipping fee is the gate fee charged to waste haulers for each ton of waste delivered to a landfill or waste facility. Higher tipping fees can incentivize recycling and waste reduction as disposal becomes more expensive.
